subitems.title: May 27, 2026 Draft Minutes Joint Work Session

meetings.raw_text: Agenda - 07/08/2026 PLANNING COMMISSION AGENDA July 8, 2026 6:30 P.M. PLANNING COMMISSION MEETINGS CAN BE VIEWED ON THE FREDERICKSBURG GOVERNMENT ACCESS CHANNEL ON COX CHANNEL 84 AND VERIZON CHANNEL 42 and www.regionalwebtv.com/fredpc . 1. Call to Order 2. Pledge Of Allegiance 3. Determination of a Quorum 4. Approval of Agenda 4.A. Agenda Documents: 2026-07-08 Agenda.pdf 5. Discussion of Potential Policies, Ordinances or Applications 6. Declaration of Conflict of Interest and Disclosures of Contact 7. Old Business 8. Public Hearing Any person may speak at a public hearing for up to five minutes. An applicant in a land use case may give an opening presentation of the case for up to 15 minutes, regardless of the number of speakers the applicant chooses to give the presentation, and may give a response after all citizens have spoken, for up to five minutes, regardless of the number of speakers the applicant chooses to give the response. The Chairman may suspend these rules in order to adapt them to any special situation. Any written comments submitted on a hearing item must include the name, address, and zip code, of the commenter, and shall be submitted by (1) email to [email protected] , (2) U.S. Mail addressed to: 601 Caroline Street, Suite 400, Fredericksburg, VA 22401, or (3) delivered to the Community Planning and Building office located at 601 Caroline Street - 4th Floor by 2:30 PM the day of the meeting. Any written comments received will be forwarded to the Planning Commission and will be available for public inspection upon request. Public comments submitted during the meeting or through the Facebook Live streaming video will not be considered part of the official public comments of the meeting. Persons requiring accommodations to facilitate participation at the public hearing or have questions are encouraged to call Planning Services at 540-372-1179 (5 days prior for accommodations). 8.A. Area 8 Dixon Street / Mayfield Small Area Plan Comprehensive Plan Amendment and Zoning Map Amendment. COMPPLAN2605-0004 / ZMA2605-0014 Comprehensive Plan Amendment to Chapters 10 and 11 of the 2025 Comprehensive Plan to adopt a Small Area Plan for Planning Area 8 Zoning Map Amendment to rezone 44 parcels, encompassing approximately 33.14 acres of land located in Planning Area 8, currently zoned as Residential-4, I-1 Light Industrial, and Commercial Highway, to the Creative Maker District and to apply form-based regulations, as recommended in the draft Small Area Plan for Area 8 Documents: 1 Staff Memo - Area 8 Small Area Plan.pdf 2 Draft Small Area 8 Comprehensive Plan Amendment.pdf 3 Appendix 1- Changes Outside of Small Area 8.pdf 4 Zoning Map Amendment Map.pdf 5 Transect and Frontage Map.pdf 9. Other Business 10. General Public Comment A general public comment period is provided at each regular meeting for comments by citizens regarding any matter related to Commission business that is not listed on the agenda for Public Hearing. The Chair will request that speakers state their name, address and zip code; observe the five-minute time limit; and yield the floor when the Clerk indicates that their time has expired. No dialogue between speakers will be permitted. Citizens who wish to make general public comments without attending the meeting will be able to send their comments in writing by (1) delivering to the Community Planning and Building office located at 601 Caroline Street - 4th Floor, (2) U.S. Mail at 601 Caroline Street, Suite 400, Fredericksburg, VA 22401, or (3) email to [email protected] . Comments must be received by 2:30 p.m. the day of the meeting. In written public comments, the writer must identify themselves by name, address, and zip code. Such comments will be forwarded to the Planning Commission and will be available for public inspection upon request. Public comments submitted during the meeting or through the Facebook Live streaming video, will not be considered part of the official public comments of the meeting. 11. Approval Of Minutes 11.A. May 27, 2026 Draft Minutes Joint Work Session Documents: 2026-05-27 Draft Minutes Joint Work Session.pdf 11.B. June 10, 2026 Regular Meeting Draft Minutes Documents: 2026-06-10 Draft Minutes Regular Meeting.pdf 12. Commissioner Comments 12.A. Chairman Comments 12.B. Planning Commissioner Comments 13. Planning Director Comments 14. Adjournment The next Planning Commission Regular Meeting is scheduled for Wednesday, August 12, 2026, at 6:30 p.m. PLANNING COMMISSION MEETINGS CAN BE VIEWED ON THE FREDERICKSBURG GOVERNMENT ACCESS CHANNEL ON COX CHANNEL 84 AND VERIZON CHANNEL 42 and www.regionalwebtv.com/fredpc .

sections.title: Approval of Officers of Election for August 4, 2026 Republican Primary

meetings.raw_text: AGENDA Electoral Board Meeting Tuesday, July 7, 2026, 5:00 pm Office of Voter Registration and Elections Fredericksburg City Center Public Speakers The Electoral Board provides this opportunity for comments from voters before the start of the meeting. Comments relevant to the registration of voters, the establishment of voting precincts, and polling places, and the conduct of the elections are welcome. To be fair to everyone, please observe the five-minute time limit and yield the floor when the Chair of the Electoral Board indicates that your time has expired. Observers At this point the Board will Open the Meeting. All speakers and those observers who have waived their right to speak are reminded that at this point you are clearly and only observers. You are not invited to interrupt nor attempt to engage Board members in discussion of Board Agenda items or other business. A. Public Comment B. Approval of the Agenda C. Approval of Minutes a. Electoral Board Meeting June 2, 2026 D. General Registrar Report E. Old Business a. August 4, 2026 Republican Primary 1. Establish Canvass Dates and Times F. New Business G. Closed Session H. Certification of Closed Session I. Approval of Officers of Election for August 4, 2026 Republican Primary J. Next Meeting Date K. Adjournment 601 Caroline Street Suite 500 Fredericksburg, VA 22401 Email: vote@vote.fredericksburgva.gov Web: www.fredericksburgVA.gov/Vote Telephone: 540-372-1030 Fax: 540-373-8381

video_clips.section_title: Main Street District Pilot Program: Street Closures and Safety
video_clips.summary: City proposes a two-year pilot program in the Main Street district to streamline event planning. Key decision: No new street closures will be approved during the pilot except for two annual events organized by Main Street, aiming to reduce bureaucratic friction for community gatherings while ensuring safety.

video_clips.section_title: Renwick Courthouse PPEA Solicitation and Public Access
video_clips.summary: Planning Director presents the Request for Proposals (RFP) for the historic Renwick Courthouse. The city seeks to transform the building into a public asset, emphasizing that 'public use or public access' is a core priority. Council reviews the scope to ensure the project serves the community rather than becoming a private luxury development.

Council reviews technical amendments to allow 'stacked' townhomes (two-over-two) in form-based codes. This change permits up to 16 units per lot, directly addressing the need for affordable, family-sized housing options in established neighborhoods without requiring large-scale redevelopment.

video_clips.section_title: 300th Anniversary Celebration: Community Engagement and Art
video_clips.summary: The 300th Anniversary Committee outlines plans for community conversations and a culminating art installation. They highlight partnerships with local organizations and the goal of using the celebration to beautify public spaces, inviting residents to engage in shaping the city's future identity.

sections.title: Ribbon Cutting Event for New Mobile Stage at Riverfront Park, 701 Sophia Street. Members of City Council, Recreation Commission, and Economic Development Authority will be in attendance.

meetings.raw_text: Agenda - 06/23/2026 City of Fredericksburg, Virginia Hon. Kerry P. Devine, Mayor Hon. Charlie L. Frye, Jr., Vice-Mayor, Ward Four Hon. Jannan W. Holmes, At-Large Hon. Dr. Will B. Mackintosh, At-Large Hon. Matt D. Rowe, Ward One Hon. Joy Y. Crump, Ward Two Hon. Susanna R. Finn, Ward Three City Council AGENDA Council Chambers 715 Princess Anne Street Fredericksburg, Virginia 22401 June 23, 2026 7:30 p.m. Mayor Kerry P. Devine, Presiding 1. Call to Order 2. Invocation Vice-Mayor Charlie L. Frye, Jr. 3. Pledge of Allegiance Councilor Jannan W. Holmes 4. Presentations 5. Consent Agenda A. Resolution 26-__, Second Read, Amending the FY 2026 Budget Supplemental Activity in the School Grants and School Capital Funds Documents: 5A Amending the FY Budget Supplemental Activity.pdf B. Transmittal of Board and Commission Minutes B.i. Architectural Review Board – May 11, 2026 Documents: Architectural Review Board Regular Meeting Minutes 2026-05-11.pdf B.ii. Board of Social Services – April 4, 2026 Documents: Board of Social Services Minutes 2026-04-04.pdf B.iii. Planning Commission – March 11, 2026 Documents: Planning Commission 2026-03-11 Regular Meeting Minutes - Approved.pdf B.iv. Economic Development Authority – May 11, 2026 Documents: Economic Development Authority Minutes Approved 2026-05-11.pdf B.v. Planning Commission – May 27, 2026 Documents: Planning Commission Regular Meeting Minutes - Approved 2026-05-27.pdf B.vi. Planning Commission CIP Committee Work Session – April 3, 2026 Documents: Planning Commission CIP Committee Work Session Minutes - Approved 2026-04-03.pdf 6. Public Hearing People who wish to participate remotely in the public hearing may send their comments in writing by 1) U.S. Mail or 2) email to the Clerk of Council. Written comments must be received no later than 1:30 p.m. on the meeting date. Written comments intended for a public hearing must be clearly identified. Written comments received on time will be read aloud during the public hearing. These rules apply to public comments: the person must identify themself by name and address, including zip code, limit remarks to five (5) minutes for speakers attending in person and three (3) minutes for submitted letters (read aloud), and comments must address a topic of City business. Decorum in the Council Chambers will be maintained. Comments that are not relevant to City business and behavior that is disruptive are inappropriate and out of order. 7. Comments from the Public City Council provides this opportunity each regular meeting for comments from people who have signed up with the Clerk of Council to speak before the start of the meeting. To be fair to everyone, please observe the five-minute time limit and yield the floor when the Clerk of Council indicates that your time has expired. Decorum in the Council Chambers will be maintained. Comments that are not relevant to City business and behavior that is disruptive are inappropriate and out of order. People who wish to participate remotely in the public comment period may send their comments in writing by 1) U.S. Mail or 2) email to the Clerk of Council. Written comments must be received no later than 1:30 p.m. on the meeting date . Written comments received on time will be read aloud during the public comment portion of the City Council meeting. These rules apply to remote public comments: the person must identify themself by name and address, including zip code, and limit remarks to three (3) minutes for submitted letters (read aloud) with a 40-minute cap on read letters. 8. Council Agenda 9. Minutes A. April 21, 2026 Budget Public Hearing Minutes Documents: 04-21-26 Regular Session Minutes Draft.pdf 10. Boards and Commission Appointments A. Economic Development Authority Appointments (2) - David Fraizer, Emisha Hall, Erica Heppe, Matthew Kelly, John Payne, and Curry Roberts Documents: 10A EDA Appt_06.23.26.pdf B. Rappahannock Regional Library Board Appointment (1) - Jeffrey Edmunds, Kenneth Lapin and Cheryl Miller Documents: 10B Library Appt.pdf C. Board of Social Services Appointment (1) - Wyanika Christophe, David Garrison, Elizabeth Kniery, Leslie Martin and Christian Zammas Documents: BSS Appt_06.23.26.pdf 11. City Manager Agenda A. Resolution 26 - __, Second Read, Authorizing an Economic Development Agreement with 2300 Fall Hill Partners, LLC and the Fredericksburg Economic Development Authority for the Redevelopment of the Property Commonly Known as the Former Mary Washington Hospital Documents: 11A EDA with 2300 Fall Hill Ave.pdf B. 300th Anniversary of Fredericksburg Blue Ribbon Steering Committee Budget Amendments Documents: Cover Memorandum for Budget Amendments for the 300th Anniversary Celebration.pdf B.i. Resolution 26 - __, First Read, Amending the FY 2026 Budget in the General Fund and the City Grants and Special Programs Fund for the Fredericksburg 300th Celebration Documents: Resolution Amending the FY 2026 Budget for the 300th Anniversary Celebration.pdf B.ii. Mr. Hughes reported that year-to-date finances are strong, with expenses under budget and income exceeding
The FY2027 draft budget was presented for discussion which focused primarily on baseline operating costs
and income. Specific areas like professional services and the Sports Tourism Fund were identified as
needing further consideration. A few members expressed concern that the Loan Program's $25,000 budget
is too low. Mr. Hughes and others suggested increasing the Loan Program’s budget to around $100,000 to

b.   InvestFXBG Loan Program – Josh Summits
Mr. Summits presented the proposed amendments and changes to the InvestFXBG credit program policy
manual, noting that the document had been reviewed and approved by legal counsel and the loan
underwriting team. Key changes include: an increase in borrowing limits to between $5,000 and $250,000,
with larger amounts considered under special circumstances, and loan terms typically priced around the
Wall Street general prime rate for three to five years, with potential for longer terms on larger cap
financing. The policy now establishes one-time and/or monthly servicing fees for closed loans. Regarding
collateral, the updated policy aims for the EDA to hold first position but allows flexibility to slide to second
position, particularly for real estate. The Loan Review Committee (LRC) composition has been formalized
to include two EDA board members and up to three non-EDA members from commercial lending
institutions within zip code 22401, with details on the appointment process and potential conflicts
addressed. Notably, the LRC's authority has shifted from full loan approval to making recommendations to
the full EDA board. Banks that have expressed interest in participating with the LRC include Shore United,
Citizens and Farmers (C&F) Bank, and First Citizens. Mr. Summits also confirmed that the Small Business
Catalyst Fund, Microloan Program, and Business Capital Growth Fund terms remain unchanged from the
MOTION by Mitzi Brown seconded by Tom Wack to adopt the proposed policies to the InvestFXBG Loan
Program: Ayes (6); Nays (0); Absent from Meeting (1 - Sullivan).
c.   Establishment of the Serve & Stay Fredericksburg Fund – Chair Schatz
Chair Schatz introduced the "Serve and Stay Program," designed to support key public service workers in
Fredericksburg. The program aims to assist first responders, teachers, and other city and school employees
with housing costs. Following a conversation with a banker specializing in workforce housing, the
proposed guidelines include mortgage rate buy-downs of up to $6,500 for home purchases and up to $4,500
for security deposits and initial rent for renters. Initially conceived for first responders and teachers, the
program has been broadened to include all City of Fredericksburg and public-school employees, while
retaining preferred qualifications for those in essential service roles.
Chair Schatz proposed an initial allocation of $30,000 to the Community Foundation to establish the fund,
emphasizing that this demonstrates public sector leadership and provides a platform for private sector
investment. The program anticipates the need for a committee, potentially composed of two EDA members,
to review and approve applications within a two-week timeframe to ensure quick turnaround for applicants
who often make employment decisions concurrently. The creation of this committee and the allocation of
funds were presented for approval, with the understanding that specific language and implementation
During the discussion, Mr. Mikityuk sought clarity around the "high need" criteria and the potential for
perceived inequity, especially when considering household incomes that could exceed typical public
servant salaries. Chair Schatz and other members explained that the intent is to support individuals offered
employment in critical roles, with the city and schools determining which roles qualify as “high need”, and
the Board making final allocation decisions based on these inputs and financial qualifications. The
discussion also touched upon the role of the Community Foundation as an intermediary for managing funds
MOTION by Mitzi Brown seconded by Anita Crossfield to allocate $30,000 from the FY2026 operating
budget to the Serve and Stay Program through the Community Foundation: Ayes (6); Nays (0); Absent from
a.   Regional Entrepreneurial Initiative Strategic Plan and Upcoming Implementation – Mike Purello
Michael Purello, head of the Rivers Region Entrepreneurial Ecosystem (RREC), provided a detailed update
on the Regional Entrepreneurial Initiative (REI) Strategic Plan and its upcoming implementation,
specifically requesting a matching fund commitment of $10,000 per year for two years from the
Fredericksburg EDA. This funding is crucial for supporting the GoVirginia implementation grant, building
upon prior support received for the planning grant.
Mr. Purello contextualized the initiative within GoVirginia Region 6, which comprises the Fredericksburg
subregion, Middle Peninsula, and Northern Neck. He highlighted that despite considerable entrepreneurial
activity and innovation ingredients; this region has not yet achieved the same level of entrepreneurial
ecosystem development as other areas. The REI Action Plan was developed through extensive research,
gap analysis, and consultations with hundreds of stakeholders across the region. The implementation grant
will focus on a subset of this comprehensive plan. The RREC aims to increase the number of
entrepreneurial startups in Region 6 and cultivate a sustainable, long-term entrepreneurial ecosystem.
Mr. Purello identified several key regional strengths, including military bases, educational institutions, and
its extensive waterways, which have inspired the "Thrive by Water" thematic approach. He also outlined
significant identified gaps, such as the underutilization of military bases, a higher-than-average veteran
population with a lower rate of veteran entrepreneurship, and limited access to venture capital and angel
investors compared to demographic and size-similar regions like Colorado Springs and Huntsville,
The implementation plan is structured around three core regional priorities: first, strengthening
entrepreneurial support by making it easier for entrepreneurs to discover the region, connect with peers, and
access expert advice; second, expanding capital access and regional networks, exploring avenues like
crowdfunding and community-based funding models; and third, leveraging regional assets such as patents
from Dahlgren, working with research institutions like VIMS, and commercializing technologies to foster
high-growth businesses. Mr. Purello emphasized that this initiative is designed to be complementary ,
rather to duplicate existing efforts, particularly those of the Fredericksburg Regional Alliance, which
focuses on attracting existing businesses and site development, while the RREC targets the attraction and
support of new entrepreneurs and startups.
Mr. Purello further elaborated on the distinction between high-growth businesses, which have the potential
for rapid scaling and global reach, and Main Street businesses. While the initiative will benefit both, the
focus on high-growth businesses is driven by GoVirginia's emphasis on economic impact and tax revenue
generation. The two-year implementation plan aims to establish a robust online presence, launch key
activities, and importantly, develop a sustainable model for the ecosystem beyond the GoVirginia funding,
likely through corporate sponsorships. The primary implementation focus will be on the Fredericksburg
subregion, with efforts to develop high-growth businesses there, while still supporting the broader region.
Regarding funding, Mr. Purello detailed existing match commitments from entities like FRA, Stafford,
Spotsylvania, King George, and Germanna, and is requesting $20,000 from the Fredericksburg EDA over
two years. The total matching funds secured will determine the amount requested from Go Virginia, with a
target of under $500,000 for GoVirginia, requiring approximately $225,000 in matching funds. The
Fredericksburg EDA will need to provide a commitment by July 10th to be included in the final
application, with a potential discussion to occur at a future meeting.
b.   EDA Grant Program Application – Amy Peregoy
An application was presented from Fredericksburg Fashion Week International requesting $2,500 in
sponsorship for the Fredericksburg Fashion Block Party, scheduled for June 6, 2026, at the Fredericksburg
Area Museum (FAM). The event is projected to attract 400 attendees and aims to enhance event
production, support local designers and artists, expand community engagement, and strengthen educational
programs, with the goal of generating measurable economic and workforce development benefits for
Fredericksburg. The applicant, Ms. Latoya White, owner of Fredericksburg Fashion Week International,
clarified that while this is a new event for Fredericksburg, her organization has a history of successful
fashion events in the area, attracting significant crowds and media attention, and has a strong track record
of placing students into higher education and reducing bullying rates in schools through fashion-focused
Ms. White explained that the requested EDA funds would primarily support production enhancements for
the block party, including lighting, backdrops, and overall visual appeal, to ensure a high-quality
representation of Fredericksburg. She noted that this event is a "block party" designed to be free and open
to the community, encouraging exploration of local businesses and shopping opportunities. While the initial
projection was for 400 attendees, organizers now anticipate 2,000-2,500 people. The event has secured
proclamations from the Mayor and participation from notable speakers like Chuck Frye. International
designers and models with substantial social media following are expected to participate, bringing their
Members raised concerns regarding the short timeline for securing sponsorships, the event's promotion, and
the clarity of vendor numbers and their confirmation. Ms. White addressed these by explaining that while
this is her first time actively seeking sponsorships from organizations like the EDA, Fredericksburg
Fashion Week International has a history of sponsorships coming to them, and that this block party event
has been promoted for nearly a year through social media, local partnerships, and media appearances,
MOTION by Kevin Hughes seconded by Chair Schatz to approve a grant in the amount of $2,500, structured
as vendor invoices, to support the Fredericksburg Fashion Week Block Party: Ayes (6); Nays (0); Absent

2026-05-27.summary: This joint work session allows city officials to review feasibility scenarios and economic impacts for the Phase III Commercial Corridor Revitalization Study. The discussion focuses on how specific development models for shopping centers might affect the local economy and urban planning goals. No binding votes are taken during this informational session; it is designed to gather input before formal decisions are made.
2026-05-27.why: Changes to commercial corridors directly influence the city's tax base by determining which businesses can operate in designated zones. These zoning adjustments affect local traffic patterns, public safety resource allocation, and the availability of goods and services for residents. The study's outcomes will shape the physical character of neighborhoods and the economic opportunities available to local families.
2026-05-27.how: Residents can attend the meeting on May 27, 2026, at 5:30 PM in the Council Chambers at 715 Princess Anne Street. Public comment periods are typically available during work sessions; check the agenda posted 48 hours prior for specific sign-up procedures. You may also submit written comments via email to the City Clerk or your council member before the meeting date.

2026-06-02.summary: The provided raw agenda data is corrupted and unreadable, preventing identification of specific items for the Fredericksburg City Council session on June 2, 2026. Consequently, no factual summary of the meeting's decisions can be generated from this input.
2026-06-02.why: Without a valid agenda, it is impossible to determine how this specific session impacts local tax bases, zoning, schools, or public safety. Residents cannot assess the relevance of the meeting to their community without knowing the actual topics under discussion.
2026-06-02.how: To engage with the Fredericksburg City Council, residents should monitor the official city website for the finalized agenda prior to the June 2, 2026 meeting. Attend the session at the Council Chambers to observe proceedings, or submit public comments according to the city's established deadlines and procedures.

2026-05-26.summary: No specific agenda items are currently published for the Fredericksburg City Council session on May 26, 2026, as the detailed agenda has not yet been released. Typically, such sessions involve the consideration of ordinances, budget approvals, or policy updates affecting city operations. Residents should check the city's official website closer to the date for the finalized list of topics.
2026-05-26.why: City Council decisions directly impact local property values, zoning regulations, and the allocation of funds for public services like schools and infrastructure. Understanding the agenda helps residents anticipate changes that may affect their neighborhood's character, tax burden, and quality of life. Engaging with these decisions ensures that community priorities are reflected in municipal governance.
2026-05-26.how: Monitor the City of Fredericksburg's official website or contact the City Clerk's office for the published agenda prior to the meeting. Attend the session at the Council Chambers to observe proceedings and submit public comments during the designated time slots. Residents may also email council members with written input regarding specific agenda items once they are available.

2026-05-12.summary: The Fredericksburg City Council will conduct first and second readings of ordinances to adjust water, sewer, and EMS fees, approve a courthouse security budget, and finalize the FY 2027 real estate tax rate. The session also includes a public hearing on a national PFAS water settlement and appointments to the Arts Commission. These actions directly impact household utility costs, property taxes, and local infrastructure funding.
2026-05-12.why: Changes to water, sewer, and EMS fees will alter monthly household expenses for residents, while the real estate tax rate of $0.84 per $100 of assessed value affects property owners' annual tax liabilities. Approving the courthouse security budget impacts public safety resources, and the PFAS settlement may influence long-term water quality and community health. These financial decisions shape the city's fiscal health and service delivery for families and businesses.
2026-05-12.how: Attend the meeting on May 12, 2026, at 7:30 p.m. in the Council Chambers at 715 Princess Anne Street to speak during the public comment period. Submit written comments via U.S. mail or email to the Clerk of Council by 1:30 p.m. on the meeting date to have them read aloud. Identify yourself by name and address, keep remarks under three minutes for written submissions, and ensure topics relate to City business.

2026-06-09.summary: The City Council will consider resolutions authorizing a joint workforce development consortium under federal law and terminating a gas rights lease at a regional landfill. The agenda also includes a first reading of amendments to the FY 2026 budget for school grants and capital funds, alongside routine approvals of board minutes and planning area processes.
2026-06-09.why: These decisions directly impact local economic development strategies and the management of regional waste infrastructure, which affects the city's tax base and environmental resources. The budget amendments influence funding levels for school grants and capital projects, determining how educational resources are allocated for the upcoming fiscal year.
2026-06-09.how: Residents can submit written comments for the public hearing by mail or email to the Clerk of Council no later than 1:30 p.m. on June 9, 2026. To speak in person, sign up with the Clerk before the meeting begins at 1:30 p.m. at 715 Princess Anne Street, adhering to the five-minute time limit and decorum rules.

2026-06-23.summary: The Fredericksburg City Council will review text amendments to the Two-Over-Twos and Form-Based Code regulations, which dictate density and building design standards. The council will also receive a report from the 300th Steering Committee regarding the city's upcoming bicentennial anniversary and consider requests for special event street closures.
2026-06-23.why: Changes to zoning codes directly impact housing supply, neighborhood character, and the long-term tax base by determining what can be built where. The 300th anniversary planning affects local business activity and public safety logistics through street closures. These decisions shape the community's physical environment and fiscal health for years to come.
2026-06-23.how: Residents can attend the session on June 23, 2026, at 5:30 PM in the Second Floor Conference Room at 715 Princess Anne Street. Public comment periods are typically available during the meeting; check the agenda for specific time limits and registration procedures. You may also email council members prior to the meeting to express your views on the zoning amendments or anniversary plans.

2026-06-25.summary: The listed agenda items indicate a biannual planning off-site for the Fredericksburg City Council, not a regular public session. This internal retreat is scheduled for June 25-26, 2026, and is not open to the public for comment or observation. Regular council meetings where binding decisions are made typically occur on separate dates and are held in the Council Chambers.
2026-06-25.why: While this specific off-site does not allow public input, the strategic plans developed during such retreats directly influence future zoning changes, tax base growth, and infrastructure investments. These long-term decisions shape the quality of local schools, public safety resource allocation, and the mix of businesses in the community. Residents should monitor subsequent regular council meetings to see how these strategic plans are translated into actionable ordinances.
2026-06-25.how: You cannot attend or speak at this off-site session as it is an internal working retreat. To engage with the council, you must attend the next regular City Council session, which is typically held in the Council Chambers at 601 Caroline Street. Check the official City of Fredericksburg website for the specific date and time of the next public meeting, where you can sign up for public comment or email council members regarding agenda items.

2026-06-26.summary: The City Council is conducting a biannual planning session off-site, not a public legislative meeting. This session is dedicated to internal strategy and long-term goal setting rather than voting on specific ordinances or budgets. No public votes or formal public comment periods are scheduled for this event.
2026-06-26.why: While this specific session does not result in immediate policy changes, the strategic plans developed here influence future zoning decisions, tax base growth, and public safety resource allocation. The outcomes will shape the city's development trajectory and service levels for local families and businesses over the coming years.
2026-06-26.how: Residents cannot attend this off-site planning session as it is closed to the public. To influence the topics discussed, contact your City Council members via email or phone before the meeting to share your priorities. Public input on specific issues will be available during regular City Council meetings held in the Council Chambers.
